coredata.dc:title: Lignin-derived green-emitting carbon quantum dots for sensitive fluorometric detection of water in ethanol
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Qiujie
Liu, Kai
Hu, Miaoyan
Gao, Shiyu
Xu, Li
Lian, Hailan
Xu, Changyan
coredata.dc:description: Efficient utilization of lignin, an abundant yet underutilized biomass resource, is essential for sustainable development and advancing functional material innovation. However, strategies for transforming lignin into practical sensing materials remain limited. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using lignin as a carbon source through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. The CQDs exhibited a fluorescence quantum yield of 15.28 % and significant solvent-dependent optical properties. Upon exposure to varying water content in ethanol, the CQDs showed a red shift in emission spectra and a decrease in fluorescence intensity due to hydrogen bonding-induced aggregation. Leveraging these properties, the CQDs were used as a sensitive fluorescent probe for detecting water content in ethanol, demonstrating a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.989) and a detection limit as low as 0.288 %. To further validate the practicality of the CQDs, their anti-interference ability was evaluated by testing the fluorescence response in the presence of common interfering solvents, including methanol, acetone, isopropanol, ethyl acetate, n-hexane, and DMSO. The results demonstrated high sensitivity and minimal interference, particularly from non-polar solvents. Furthermore, the sensing platform was converted into a portable paper-based sensor for convenient, real-time detection of water content in ethanol under UV light. This study presents a sustainable approach for lignin utilization and offers a promising tool for monitoring water content in ethanol and potentially other organic solvents, even in complex environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Carbon quantum dots
Ethanol
Water determination
Paper-based sensor

Detecting water in ethanol with paper based CQDs sensors
Anti-interference performance of the fluorescence method
Morphology and structure of the CQDs
Detecting water in ethanol with the prepared CQDs solution sensor
Detecting water in ethanol with paper based CQDs sensors
Anti-interference performance of the fluorescence method
Mechanism and novelty of detecting water in ethanol with paper-based CQDs sensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of CQDs. (c) Size distribution histograms of CQDs.
FTIR spectra of lignin fluorescent CQDs.
(a) Full survey XPS spectrum of lignin-based CQDs, (b) high-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s，(c) N1s and (d) O1s.

(a) FL emission spectra excited at the wavelength of 410 to 470 nm, (b) UV–vis absorption, optimal FL excitation and emission spectra, (c) FL emission spectra with the ultraviolet irradiation duration, (d) fluorescence decay curve of the CQDs fitted by two-order exponential functions.

(a) Fluorescence photos of CQDs dispersed in Ethanol with different water contents, (b) Chromaticity color coordinates in the CIE diagram of CQDs dispersed in Ethanol with increasing water content from 0 % to 80 % at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, (c) FL spectra of the CQDs for detection of water content from 0 to 80 % in Ethanol, (d) The linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity and water content.
(a) CQDs test paper at different ethanol moisture contents under 365 nm UV lamp. (b) Comparison of Calibration Curves: Solution vs Paper-Based Detection.

head.ce:title: Lignin-derived green-emitting carbon quantum dots for sensitive fluorometric detection of water in ethanol

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient utilization of lignin, an abundant yet underutilized biomass resource, is essential for sustainable development and advancing functional material innovation. However, strategies for transforming lignin into practical sensing materials remain limited. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using lignin as a carbon source through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. The CQDs exhibited a fluorescence quantum yield of 15.28 % and significant solvent-dependent optical properties. Upon exposure to varying water content in ethanol, the CQDs showed a red shift in emission spectra and a decrease in fluorescence intensity due to hydrogen bonding-induced aggregation. Leveraging these properties, the CQDs were used as a sensitive fluorescent probe for detecting water content in ethanol, demonstrating a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.989) and a detection limit as low as 0.288 %. To further validate the practicality of the CQDs, their anti-interference ability was evaluated by testing the fluorescence response in the presence of common interfering solvents, including methanol, acetone, isopropanol, ethyl acetate, n-hexane, and DMSO. The results demonstrated high sensitivity and minimal interference, particularly from non-polar solvents. Furthermore, the sensing platform was converted into a portable paper-based sensor for convenient, real-time detection of water content in ethanol under UV light. This study presents a sustainable approach for lignin utilization and offers a promising tool for monitoring water content in ethanol and potentially other organic solvents, even in complex environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is an essential substance in everyday life and is also considered a contaminant or impurity in organic solvents [1,2]. In some sensitive chemical reactions and industrial production, water can lead to reaction termination, resulting in low reaction yields and difficulties in experimental reproducibility, and even trigger catastrophic accidents such as fires and explosions [3–5]. Given its importance in chemical reaction, many analytical methods have been developed to monitor and quantify the water content of organic solvents. Karl Fischer titration is widely used as a classical method for determination of water in organic solvents [6]. However, the disadvantages of using toxic reagents and not being able to monitor in real time limit the application of this method. Some other techniques, for detecting water content in organic solvents, such as gas chromatography [7] and nuclear magnetic resonance [8], are also available. But they also have some disadvantages that to some extent prevent them from becoming universal methods for water content detection in organic solvents, such as the need for expensive instruments, time consuming and trained operators.

Fluorescence analysis shows more advantages due to its simple operation, rapid response, low cost, and in situ monitoring. Many luminescent probes or nanomaterials have been designed for water sensing, such as organic molecules [9,10], nanoclusters [11], metal-organic frameworks [12], covalent organic frameworks [13] and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [14–16], have been designed for water sensing in organic solvents. CQDs stands out for its unique advantages, such as excellent optical stability, low toxicity, simple preparation and easy functionalization. In 2010, it was found that CQDs had solvent-dependent characteristics [17]; for example, its spectra of UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence fluorescence (FL) in acetone showed a red shift compared to that observed in water This work opens a new idea and direction for the detection of water in organic solvents. Since then, a number of CQDs with solvent-dependent characteristics for water sensing in organic solvents have sprung up [18–20].

Green luminescent CQDs was obtained by Chao et al. through hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine. The fluorescence intensity of this CQDs in acetone gradually decreases with increasing water content in acetone (>40 %) and the emission color changes from green to yellow [21]. Wang's team synthesized solvent dependent CQDs and realized visual monitoring of water in a variety of organic solvents [22]. Using CQDs to detect water content in organic solvents has become a research hotspot, but there are still some problems. For example, most of the precursors for the synthesis of CQDs are small molecules, which are not environmentally friendly and not conducive to large-scale application. Therefore, it is of great importance to synthesize environmentally friendly and biocompatible CQDs for the detection of water content in organic solvents.

As the second largest natural biomass resource after cellulose in nature, lignin has a high carbon content, complex aromatic skeletal structure, and high oxygen-containing functional group branching [23,24]. However, the value of lignin has not been widely developed. It is considered as a by-product of waste in pulp industry and cellulosic ethanol bioreneries, of which <2 % is reused in industrial production and the rest is directly burned [25]. It is not only wastes resources, but also causes serious environmental pollution. Researchers have found that lignin contributes to the formation of CQDs based on its surface-functionalized conjugated graphite-based nuclei [26]. Using lignin as a carbon source to develop environmentally friendly carbon quantum dots is a new idea for efficient utilization of lignin. In fact, there have been attempts to obtain blue luminescent CQDs using alkaline lignin as a carbon source, which have been successfully used for cellular imaging and Fe3+ detection in water [27,28]. However, the fluorescence quantum yield of lignin based CQDs in the above studies is low, usually <10 %. Moreover, few studies have involved carbon quantum dots derived from lignin as fluorescence sensors to detect water content in organic solvents. Therefore, it is still a challenge to synthesize environmentally friendly carbon quantum dots with high fluorescence quantum yield using lignin as a carbon source by a simple and environmentally friendly process and successfully apply it to the detection of water in organic solvents.

In this paper, a type of solvent dependent CQDs with a fluorescence quantum yield of 15.28 % was prepared by one-step hydrothermal method using lignin as a precursor as well as m-phenylenediamine (MPDA) and HCl as dopants. Taking ethanol, a type of common organic solvent laboratory, as the representative, the different responses of the CQDs to different water content in ethanol were systematically studied. In addition, a portable paper based CQDs sensor was also fabricated in this study, which is expected to achieve convenient, real-time, and visual effects in the detection of water content in ethanol.

Lignin and HCl were purchased from Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.(Shanghai, China) and Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. respectively; MPDA, Quinine sulfate (99.0 %), methanol, acetone, isopropanol, ethyl acetate, n-hexane, and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were not further purified. Deionized water with a (resistivity of 18.25 MΩ) was home-made in the laboratory.

CQDs was prepared by hydrothermal methodusing lignin as a carbon source [29], as well as MPDA and HCl as dopants. Lignin (0.4 g) and MPDA (0.6 g) were mixed well with deionized water (45 mL) in a beaker, then hydrochloric acid (1 mL) was introduced into the mixture. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lining (100 mL) with stainless steel autoclave, which was placed into an electric oven at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the dark brown reaction mixture was poured out from the autoclave. To remove unreacted impurities, the mixture was filtered with a piece of microporous membrane (pore size, 0.22 μm) and centrifuged (10,000 rpm for 15 min), and then the supernatant of centrifugal treatment was subjected to dialysis (MWCO 1000, 48 h). The remaining solution after dialysis treatment is CQDs solution. Solid CQDs for subsequent characterization of CQDs was obtained by vacuum freeze-drying at −65 °C for 72 h.

Morphological characterization of the CQDs was performed on a High resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, JEM-2100 UHR Japan), which was operated at 200 kV using an ultra-thin carbon film coated copper grating.

The laser Raman spectrometer (DXR532, Thermo Fisher, USA) was set with the following parameters: an excitation wavelength of 780 nm, a power of 10 mw, a slit width of 50 μm, and a measurement range of 500–2250 cm−1. The crystalline structure of the CQDs was determined by comparing the proportion of the D band (representing sp2 amorphous structures) and the G band (representing sp2 structures) in the Raman spectrum.

The functional groups on the surface of CQDs was characterized by a Fourier infrared spectrometer (Nicolet IS10, Thermo Fisher, USA). The test wavenumber range was 4000–500 cm−1, and the test mode was set to “Smart iTR diamond ATR”.

The CQDs' elemental composition and bonding states were analyzed using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (AXIS-Ultra DLD, Shimadzu, Japan). The optical properties of the CQDs were assessed by ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum, photoluminescence fluorescence (FL) spectrum, as well as photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and fluorescence lifetime. The spectra of UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission was recorded by an UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, Lambda 950, USA) and a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, LS55, USA), respectively. For the photoluminescence emission test with LS 55 instrument, the slit widths for excitation and emission were set at 15 nm and 2.5 nm, respectively.

The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) is the number of emitted photons relative to the number of absorbed photons. PLQY was calculated via the formula (1) [30]. Quinine sulfate, which was dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid, was used as the reference with PLQY of 54 % (excited at 360 nm).
(1)
Q X
=
Q R
∙
I X
/
I R
∙
A R
/
A X
∙
η X 2
/
η R 2
where, “Q” is the PLQY of the prepared CQDs. “I” stands for the integrated emission intensity, which is calculated from the emission spectra at 360 nm excitation. “A” represents the UV–vis absorbance at 360 nm, which should be controlled under 0.05 in the 10 mm quartz absorbance cell to avoid re-absorption. η represents the refractive index of solvent (η = 1.33,
η X 2
/
η R 2
=1). The subscripts X and R correspond to the CQDs sample and the reference, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting water in ethanol with the prepared CQDs solution sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
First, ethanol solution with different water content was prepared according to Table 1 , and was added into the CQDs solution (1 mg/mL). Then, a fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to evaluate the sensitivity of the CQDs fluorescence probe to water in ethanol. The emission intensity of the mixture of CQDs solution and ethanol solution was recorded under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm UV light. The detection limit (LOD) of the CQDs fluorescence probe to water in ethanol was calculated by formula (2) [31].
(2)
LOD =
3 σ
s
where σ means the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensities of CQDs, and S stands for the slope of the linear curve fitted. Under the same experimental conditions, the fluorescence intensity of these blank samples was measured several times. The fluorescence intensity values of each measurement were recorded, and a set of data was obtained: [F1,2,F3,...,Fn]. Calculate the standard deviation using the formula (3):
(3)
σ =
1
n − 1
∑
i = 1
n
Fi − ‾ F
2
where Fi is the fluorescence intensity measured at the first i,
F ¯
is the average of all measurements, and n is the number of measurements. The standard deviation (σ=0.1002 %) was obtained from 10 independent measurements of blank samples under identical conditions. The slope (S) was derived from the linear regression of the calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting water in ethanol with paper based CQDs sensors
ce:section.ce:para: For the convenience of storage and operation, we also made paper-based test strips with the prepared CQDs for water detection in ethanol. First, rectangular paper strips (20 mm × 10 mm), which were cut from a piece of cellulose filter paper(Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Nanjing, China), were soaked in the prepared CQDs solution (1 mg/mL) for 2 h. Then, air dry the test strips at room temperature until the remaining water in them has completely evaporated. And then, the prepared test strips were immersed in ethanol solution with different water content (0,5 %,10 %,30 %,50 %,80 %), dried in air at room temperature, and then irradiated under 365 nm UV light. The images showing fluorescence change of the test strips were immediately captured using the camera on a smartphone(Huawei, China). Then, the sensitivity of the paper-based CQDs sensor to water content in ethanol was tested using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The LOD of the paper-based CQDs sensor for water content in ethanol was studied, and the calibration curve for paper-based detection was plotted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference performance of the fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the anti-interference ability of the CQDs-based fluorescence sensing system, common solvents such as methanol, acetone, isopropanol, ethyl acetate, n-hexane, and dimethyl sulfoxide were selected as interfering substances. Each solvent was mixed with ethanol-water solutions containing a known water content, while the concentration of the interfering solvent was maintained at 2 % (v/v). The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs in the presence of these solvents was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer under identical experimental conditions, with the ethanol-water system without interfering solvents serving as the control. Each measurement was repeated three times to ensure reproducibility. The relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) was calculated, where F represents the fluorescence intensity in the presence of the interfering solvent and F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of the control sample. A bar chart was plotted to compare the relative fluorescence intensity under different interfering solvents, providing a comprehensive evaluation of the system's robustness. Minimal changes in F/F0 indicated strong anti-interference performance, whereas significant deviations suggested interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of the CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the morphology of the prepared CQDs investigated by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy. As shown in Fig. 1a, the particles of the CQDs are sphere-like and well dispersed in the aqueous solution without obvious aggregation. The average particle size of the CQDs is 4.42 nm (Fig. 1c), and its particle size is mainly distributed in the range from 3 to 7 nm. In addition, the CQDs possess lattice fringes spaced at 0.21 nm, corresponding to the in-plane lattice spacing of graphene (100 facet) [32], indicating that the prepared CQDs contain a large amount of graphite-like structure in the carbon core. In conclusion, the above results prove that CQDs can be obtained using lignin as a carbon source by simple hydrothermal method [33].

The spectra of FT-IR and XPS were used to speculate functional groups and chemical structure of the obtained carbon quantum dots, as shown in Figs. 2 and 3
.

To further investigate the structural characteristics of the synthesized lignin-derived CQDs, Raman spectroscopy was performed. As shown in Fig. 2, the Raman spectrum exhibits two prominent peaks: the D band at approximately 1350 cm−1 and the G band at 1580 cm−1. The D band corresponds to the disordered carbon structure, which is attributed to defects and amorphous carbon present in the CQDs. In contrast, the G band represents the graphitic sp2 hybridized carbon framework, indicative of ordered graphitic domains within the CQDs [34]. The relative intensity ratio of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) was calculated to be approximately 0.92, suggesting that the CQDs possess a moderate degree of defects while maintaining significant graphitic characteristics. The presence of defects in the CQDs plays a crucial role in their optical and sensing properties. The defects, such as vacancies and functional group modifications (e.g., nitrogen doping introduced during synthesis), enhance the surface reactivity and increase the density of fluorescence active sites. These characteristics are pivotal for the CQDs' application in fluorescence-based sensing of water content. Additionally, the combination of graphitic and disordered carbon structures provides a balance between stability and functionality. The graphitic domains contribute to the structural robustness of the CQDs, while the disordered regions introduce tunable optical properties. This structural duality highlights the versatility of lignin-derived CQDs for practical sensing applications.

In Fig. 3, there is a weak absorption peak at 3320 cm−1, which is associated with stretching vibrations of OH or NH [35]. The peak at 3124 cm−1 is derived from stretching vibrations of NH, and the broad peak at 2802 cm−1 is due to CH stretching and bending vibrations [15]. The peak at 2587 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of -OH. peaks near 1524 cm−1 and 1401 cm−1 correspond to the characteristic absorption of the chemical bond CC and C-C/C=C [36], respectively, indicating the presence of sp2 structures in CQDs, mainly due to the increase in the amount of conjugated graphitic carbon in the lignin carbon sites during hydrothermal processes, which is consistent with the HRTEM results. Furthermore, 1146 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibration of the CO [37].

XPS spectra were used to investigate the composition and valance state of the CQDs. In Fig. 4a. the full XPS spectrum of the CQDs exhibits three obvious peaks 284.8 eV, 401 eV, and 533 eV, which are ascribed to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s the CQDs, respectively. It reveals that the CQDs contain the elements of C, N, and O,and indicates the successful incorporation of N atom into/ onto the carbon framework of CQDs [38].

Fig. 4b–d displays the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, and O1s. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 4b) was divided into three binding energy peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.7 eV and, 289 eV, which are attributed to C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N and -COOH, respectively [39,40]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) was deconvoluted into two signal peaks at 399.6 eV and 401.6 eV, corresponding to C-N-C and NH bonds, respectively [41,42]. In the O1s divided spectra (Fig. 4d), two peaks appear at 531.8 eV and 533.1 eV, which are ascribed to CO and CO bonds, respectively [43]. Those findings suggest that the CQDs are enriched in nitrogen and oxygen, which confirms the results of the FTIR analysis described above. So, a conclusion can be drawn that the surface of the prepared CQDs in this study contains a variety of groups, such as OH, -COOH, and NH, playing a key role in the excellent fluorescence properties of the CQDs and the successful application in detecting water in ethanol.

Fig. 5 shows the emission spectra of the prepared lignin-based fluorescent CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (410 nm ~ 470 nm). In Fig. 5a, the CQDs displays a broad emission spectrum (from blue to near infrared) and shows green fluorescence. In the excitation wavelength range of 410–470 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs increases first and then decreases, and reaches the maximum at the excitation wavelength of 440 nm (optimal FL excitation). The corresponding emission wavelength is approximately 510 nm (optimal FL emission), which exhibits excitation wavelength independence. This is attributed to the conjugation effect of the functional groups and nuclei on the surface of the CQDs, resulting in relatively homogeneous size of the dots and the same surface emission position.

The PLQY of the obtained lignin-based CQDs was calculated as 15.28 % using Eq. (2), which is higher than that of biomass-derived carbon quantum dots reported in previous studies [44]. This improvement can be attributed to the presence of m-phenylenediamine, which is rich in nitrogen. During the hydrothermal process, a large number of N-containing groups are introduced into the carbon core structure and onto the edge of the sp2− hybridized structure of the CQDs. These nitrogen-related surface chemical states effectively increase the surface electron cloud density and enrich the fluorescence-active sites, thereby significantly enhancing the electron-hole radiative recombination probability. As a result, the m-phenylenediamine-doped CQDs exhibit a higher fluorescence quantum yield.

Fig. 5b shows the CQDs' spectra of UV–vis absorption (the blue line), optimal FL excitation (the green line), and emission (the orange line). The CQDs has optical absorption capacity in the ultraviolet region, and the absorption peaks are mainly distributed in the wavelength range of 250 to 300 nm, among which the highest absorption is at 274 nm, which is attributed to π–π* electron leap of the CC bond in the CQDs [15]. This result is consistent with other literature reports [45,46]. In addition, the spectra of the optical FL excitation (440 nm) and emission (510 nm) also confirms the green luminescence performance of the CQDs.

The photostability of fluorescence materials is important for practical applications. So, the photostability of the prepared CQDs was investigated, as shown in Fig. 5c. The fluorescence emission intensity of the CQDs did not change significantly after continuous exposure to 365 nm UV light for 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120 min with an xenon lamp (500 W). This result indicates that the CQDs has good fluorescence stability and photobleaching resistance, and is a good fluorescent probe for analytical assays.

To gain insight into the optical performance, the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs was also investigated, as displayed in Fig. 5d. The average lifetime of the CQDs was 4.59 ns. Such a short lifetime suggests that the luminescence of the CQDs can be caused by a defective state on the surface of the CQDs, and these surface states can lead to the compounding of electrons and holes, which then releases energy in the form of photoemission [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting water in ethanol with the prepared CQDs solution sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the polarity dependence of the CQDs aqueous solution, we tried to use it as a fluorescent probe to detect the water content in ethanol. As shown in Fig. 6a, as the proportion of water in ethanol increases from 0 to 80 %, the CQDs' color shows a distinct visual change from blue to cyan and then to green when illuminated by an UV lamp (365 nm), which is due to the water in ethanol increases, the polarity of the ethanol/water/CQDs mixture becomes greater and the hydrogen bond between water and CQDs/CQDs and CQDs becomes stronger, leading to the aggregation of adjacent CQDs particles. This aggregation increases the π-π interactions and resonance energy transfer of CQDs, decreasing the emission energy level of CQDs, causing the emission spectrum of CQDs to be red-shifted and the fluorescence to be attenuated. The CIE diagram in Fig. 6b also demonstrates this color change as the water content in ethanol increases from 0 to 80 %. The fluorescence spectrogram (Fig. 6c) of the CQDs proves that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs gradually decreased, and the blue peak located near 420 nm gradually weakened until it disappeared. Moreover, a good linear relationship was found between the fluorescence intensity and water ratio in the range of 5–80 %(Fig. 6d). The linear fitting equation is y = −1.0439x + 216.9604(R2 = 0.989),and the LOD was calculated to be 0.288 % according to Eq. (2). This meets the criterion of no >0.3 % water content in ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting water in ethanol with paper based CQDs sensors
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the practicality of the CQDs in detecting water in ethanol, we attempted to fabricate solid CQDs as a luminescent material. As shown in Fig. 7(a), as the water content in ethanol gradually increases from 0 5 to 80 %, the color of the strips changes from blue to cyan and finally to green, showing a clear visualization effect. This phenomenon is almost identical to that in Fig. 7(a) and illustrates the promising application of this portable paper-based sensor for detecting water in ethanol. To further validate the practical application of CQDs in paper-based sensors, we compared the calibration curves of paper-based detection and solution-based detection, as shown in Fig. 7(b). The fluorescence intensity in solution-based detection exhibited a clear linear decline with increasing water content, demonstrating high sensitivity and a low detection limit. The solution-based detection exhibits a strong linear relationship (R2 = 0.989) with higher sensitivity (slope = −1.0439) and lower variability. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity in paper-based detection also showed a decreasing trend with increasing water content, but with slightly reduced sensitivity (slope = −1.0126) and increased variability. This difference can be attributed to two main factors. First, the uneven distribution of CQDs on the paper substrate may lead to a partial loss of fluorescence signal. Second, the weak absorption of fluorescence signals by the paper-based material affects detection sensitivity. Despite these limitations, the comparison between paper-based and solution-based detection highlights the versatility and practicality of CQDs as moisture detection sensors. While paper-based sensors are slightly less sensitive than their solution-based counterparts, their portability, ease of use, and suitability for rapid, on-site moisture detection make them an effective and promising alternative for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference performance of the fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the anti-interference ability of the fluorescence method, the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of CQDs was measured in the presence of various interfering solvents, as shown in Fig. 8 . Here, F 0 represents the fluorescence intensity of the control group (ethanol-water system). The solvents tested include methanol [48], acetone [49], isopropanol [50], ethyl acetate [51], n-hexane [52], and DMSO [51], which are commonly encountered in potential application environments. The results indicate that the relative fluorescence intensity varies depending on the solvent. For Methanol and Isopropanol, these solvents exhibited moderate interference, reducing F/F0 to 0.95 and 0.90, respectively. This suggests that their similar polarity and hydrogen-bonding ability to ethanol contribute to their interference with the fluorescence signal. Acetone and Ethyl Acetate caused significant reductions in F/F0 (0.85 and 0.88, respectively), likely due to their higher polarity disrupting the interaction between CQDs and the water-ethanol matrix. As a non-polar solvent, n-hexane showed minimal interference, with F/F0 remaining at 0.98. This highlights the robustness of the CQDs fluorescence in the presence of non-polar solvents. DMSO's high polarity and strong hydrogen-bonding ability likely compete with water molecules, altering the fluorescence response. The fluorescence intensity decreased significantly (F/F0 = 0.8). The findings demonstrate that the CQDs fluorescence method exhibits acceptable anti-interference performance in the presence of low to moderately polar solvents. However, high-polarity solvents like DMSO pose significant challenges, as they disrupt the fluorescence mechanism by interacting with the CQDs and altering their surface states.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism and novelty of detecting water in ethanol with paper-based CQDs sensors
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the practicality and novelty of CQDs in detecting water in ethanol, we fabricated a solid-state paper-based CQDs sensor. When illuminated under 365 nm UV light, the sensor demonstrates a distinct visual change with increasing water content, from blue to cyan and finally to green (Fig. 7a), achieving a clear and intuitive visualization effect.

The novelty of the prepared CQDs lies in the combination of lignin as a biomass precursor and the nitrogen-rich dopant m-phenylenediamine (MPDA), which was synthesized using a simple hydrothermal method. This approach introduces nitrogen-containing groups into the CQDs, such as NH and C-N-C, improving the fluorescence efficiency and stability. Compared to previous methods using small-molecule carbon sources, this process is not only eco-friendly but also cost-effective for large-scale production.

The unique sensing mechanism stems from solvent polarity and hydrogen-bonding effects. As the water content increases in ethanol, the polarity of the ethanol/water mixture rises, leading to the aggregation of adjacent CQDs. This aggregation strengthens the π-π interactions and resonance energy transfer, reducing the emission energy levels and increasing non-radiative decay pathways [15]. As a result, the emission spectrum shifts to longer wavelengths (redshift), and the fluorescence intensity weakened to traditional fluorescent probes or methods such as gas chromatography and titration, the CQDs sensor offers three major advantages:
(1) Rapid visual detection: The color change from blue to green provides immediate and real-time feedback.
(2) High sensitivity: A linear relationship between water content (0–80 %) and fluorescence intensity was achieved with a detection limit of 0.288 %.
(3) Portability and ease of use: The paper-based sensor simplifies the operation and enables practical applications in field testing.

In summary, the integration of lignin-derived CQDs with nitrogen doping and the portable paper-based design highlights their novelty and practicality for water sensing. This work paves the way for the development of eco-friendly, efficient, and low-cost sensors for detecting water content in ethanol and potentially other organic solvents.

In this study, lignin-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized via a simple and environmentally friendly hydrothermal method using m-phenylenediamine as a nitrogen dopant. The CQDs exhibited a fluorescence quantum yield of 15.28 % and solvent-dependent optical behavior, enabling their use as a sensitive fluorescent probe for detecting water content in ethanol. The solution-based detection demonstrated a strong linear relationship (R2 = 0.989) and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.288 %.

To evaluate their practicality, anti-interference tests were conducted with common solvents such as methanol, acetone, isopropanol, and DMSO. The CQDs showed high robustness with minimal fluorescence variation under non-polar solvents and moderate tolerance to polar solvents, supporting their reliability in complex environments. Additionally, the CQDs were incorporated into a paper-based sensor, providing a portable platform for real-time and on-site water detection, though with slightly reduced sensitivity compared to the solution-based method.

This study demonstrates the high-value utilization of lignin and highlights the potential of CQDs as a sustainable and versatile sensing platform for water content detection in ethanol and other solvent systems.

Qiujie Gao: Writing – review & editing. Kai Liu: Writing – original draft. Miaoyan Hu: Data curation. Shiyu Gao: Formal analysis. Li Xu: Validation. Hailan Lian: Supervision. Changyan Xu: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu Y, Sun D, Zhang Z, et al. Amphiphilic Carbon Dots with Excitation-Independent Double-Emissions[J]. Particle & Particle Systems Characterization, 2020, 37(7): 2000146.
